title-group.article-title: Nonpharmacologic Care of Bronchiectasis: Addressing Frailty with Nutrition and Physical Activity

contrib-group.aff: 1Providence South Sound Pulmonary and Sleep Medicine, 500 Lilly Road Northeast, Suite 204, Olympia, WA 98506 USA
2https://ror.org/04vfsmv21grid.410305.30000 0001 2194 5650National Institutes of Health-Clinical Center, Bethesda, MD USA

license.license-p: Open Access This article is licensed under a Creative Commons Attribution-NonCommercial 4.0 International License, which permits any non-commercial use, sharing, adaptation, distribution and reproduction in any medium or format, as long as you give appropriate credit to the original author(s) and the source, provide a link to the Creative Commons licence, and indicate if changes were made. The images or other third party material in this article are included in the article's Creative Commons licence, unless indicated otherwise in a credit line to the material. If material is not included in the article's Creative Commons licence and your intended use is not permitted by statutory regulation or exceeds the permitted use, you will need to obtain permission directly from the copyright holder. To view a copy of this licence, visit http://creativecommons.org/licenses/by-nc/4.0/.

sec.title: Introduction
sec.p: Bronchiectasis is a chronic airway disease marked by recurrent infections, progressive inflammation, and declining pulmonary function. While pharmacologic therapies remain central to management, nonpharmacologic strategies—particularly nutrition and physical activity—are underutilized, despite growing evidence.

sec.title: Objective
sec.p: This review examines the role of nutritional support and physical activity in managing bronchiectasis, highlighting their impact on frailty, systemic inflammation, and functional outcomes.

sec.title: Discussion
sec.p: Frailty is increasingly recognized in bronchiectasis, particularly in patients with comorbid nontuberculous mycobacterial lung disease (NTM-LD), where prevalence may exceed 40%. Malnutrition, low body mass index (BMI), and sarcopenia are associated with poorer lung function, increased hospitalizations, and mortality. Meanwhile, physical inactivity—measured by low step count and prolonged sedentary time—is a strong predictor of exacerbation risk and healthcare utilization. Evidence supports the use of high-calorie, protein-rich diets and resistance-based exercise training to improve muscle mass, immune function, and quality of life. Synergistic effects are observed when nutritional interventions are combined with pulmonary rehabilitation. However, barriers to implementation remain, including a lack of access, under-referral, and limited emphasis on guidelines.

sec.title: Conclusion
sec.p: Nutritional and physical activity interventions offer measurable clinical benefits in bronchiectasis and should be integrated into routine multidisciplinary care. Future research should prioritize the validation of frailty screening tools, the implementation of effective strategies, and the development of policy mechanisms to expand coverage for dietetic and rehabilitation services.

custom-meta.meta-value: © Springer Healthcare Ltd., part of Springer Nature 2026

tr.td: Frailty, malnutrition, and physical inactivity are common in bronchiectasis and strongly associated with worse outcomes, including reduced lung function and higher hospitalization risk.
Patients with low BMI show impaired lung function, while patients with low daily activity or prolonged sedentary behavior face markedly increased risk of exacerbation-related admissions.
Nutritional support and structured physical activity, especially when integrated with pulmonary rehabilitation, improve muscle mass, immune function, and quality of life.

Despite growing evidence, these interventions remain underutilized; future efforts must validate frailty screening tools and expand policy support for nutrition and rehabilitation services in bronchiectasis care.

sec.title: Introduction
sec.p: Bronchiectasis is a heterogeneous and underrecognized chronic respiratory disorder characterized by irreversible dilation of the bronchi, impaired mucociliary clearance, and persistent airway inflammation. The disease is marked by chronic productive cough, recurrent pulmonary infections, and progressive decline in lung function. While bronchiectasis can result from a variety of underlying etiologies, including post-infectious damage, immune deficiencies, and ciliary dysfunction, many cases are idiopathic. The disease trajectory is heterogeneous, ranging from mild symptoms to frequent exacerbations and significant respiratory morbidity. In recent years, there has been increasing recognition of its rising global prevalence and associated healthcare burden, especially among older adults and those with comorbidities such as chronic obstructive pulmonary disease (COPD) and nontuberculous mycobacterial lung disease (NTM-LD).

Management of bronchiectasis remains multifaceted, involving airway clearance techniques, antibiotic therapy for exacerbations of chronic infections, routine vaccinations, and treatment of underlying causes. Pharmacologic interventions, including macrolides and inhaled antibiotics, have demonstrated benefit in reducing exacerbation frequency and microbial load. However, these treatments do not fully address the systemic consequences of the disease, such as physical deconditioning, malnutrition, and frailty, factors increasingly associated with worse clinical outcomes.

Frailty is emerging as a significant and underrecognized comorbidity in bronchiectasis. Defined as a state of decreased physiologic reserve and increased vulnerability to stressors, frailty is prevalent in 22% of patients with bronchiectasis and nearly 50% of those with NTM-LD [1]. It is associated with reduced exercise capacity, impaired quality of life, and higher rates of hospitalization and mortality. Two of the most modifiable contributors to frailty—malnutrition and physical inactivity—are highly prevalent in bronchiectasis populations. Underweight status (body mass index [BMI] < 18.5 kg/m2) is independently associated with lower forced expiratory volume in 1 s (FEV1) and increased mortality [2], while sedentary behavior and reduced physical activity correlate with more frequent exacerbations and hospital admissions [3]. These findings underscore the need to broaden the scope of bronchiectasis management beyond pharmacologic control to include nutritional rehabilitation and exercise-based interventions.

Improved nutritional strategies, when combined with structured physical activity—particularly resistance training or pulmonary rehabilitation—offer additive benefits in improving functional capacity and quality of life. However, implementation remains inconsistent. Dietitian services are not routinely involved in bronchiectasis care, and pulmonary rehabilitation is underutilized, particularly in frail patients who may benefit the most. While pulmonary rehabilitation and dietary counseling are established in chronic obstructive pulmonary disease (COPD) and other chronic lung diseases, they are not yet standard practice guidelines in bronchiectasis. This gap highlights the need for a paradigm shift toward more holistic, multimodal management approaches that address the systemic drivers of disease progression and patient decline.

This review aims to synthesize current evidence on the role of nonpharmacologic interventions—specifically nutrition and physical activity—in the management of bronchiectasis. We examine the underlying pathophysiological rationale linking malnutrition, sarcopenia, and sedentary behavior to the progression of bronchiectasis. We evaluate the impact of nutritional status and physical activity on disease outcomes, review the evidence for dietary and exercise interventions, and explore strategies for integrating these approaches into clinical care. The review also discusses future research directions, implementation challenges, and policy implications for the broader adoption of comprehensive care models. This article is based on previously conducted studies and does not contain any new studies with human participants or animals performed by any of the authors.

sec.title: Pathophysiologic Rationale
sec.p: In addition to its respiratory effects, there is a growing body of evidence demonstrating that frailty—a syndrome marked by diminished physiological reserve and increased vulnerability—is a common and significant comorbidity that must be addressed in clinical settings. Frailty in bronchiectasis results from a complex interplay of systemic inflammation, malnutrition, sarcopenia, and physical inactivity, each reinforcing the other to create a cycle of functional decline.

At the molecular level, persistent neutrophilic inflammation characteristic of bronchiectasis drives the release of pro-inflammatory cytokines such as interleukin-6 (IL-6) and tumor necrosis factor-alpha (TNF-α). These cytokines activate catabolic pathways, impair muscle-building (myogenesis), and blunt anabolic signals, including insulin-like growth factor-1 (IGF-1) [4, 5]. Over time, this environment leads to muscle wasting, fatigue, and reduced exercise tolerance, core features of frailty. As muscle mass and strength decline—particularly in the respiratory musculature—the ability to clear mucus diminishes, setting the stage for repeated infections and further deterioration.

Sarcopenia is an age-related, multifactorial process that is characterized by the loss of muscle mass. While the onset of sarcopenia is insidious, progression may be significantly accelerated by poor nutrition and physical inactivity. After the age of 30 years, adults typically lose 3–8% of their muscle mass per decade [6, 7]. Loss of muscle mass related to inactivity is thought to disproportionately affect the lower body musculature, with the most pronounced changes often observed during the early days to weeks of inactivity, largely on the basis of extrapolated evidence outside of bronchiectasis [8].

sec.title: Malnutrition and Muscle Loss
sec.p: Malnutrition is a key driver of frailty and functional decline in individuals with bronchiectasis. Exacerbations and chronic inflammation contribute to anorexia, early satiety, and increased metabolic demands, leading to a sustained negative energy balance. Protein-energy malnutrition, in particular, accelerates loss of lean body mass and compromises immune competence, respiratory muscle strength, and mucociliary clearance.

Low body mass index (BMI), a widely used surrogate marker for nutritional status, has been independently associated with worse outcomes in bronchiectasis. Patients with a BMI < 18.5 kg/m2 are more likely to exhibit lower FEV1, greater radiologic severity, and increased mortality risk [2]. Notably, underweight patients are also more frequently colonized by pathogenic organisms and demonstrate phenotypes consistent with post-infective or nontuberculous mycobacteria (NTM)-related disease [1].

Micronutrient deficiencies further compound these risks. Inadequate levels of vitamin D, iron, and antioxidants impair immune defenses and reduce respiratory muscle performance. Vitamin D, in particular, has been linked to reduced muscle strength, impaired lung function, and higher rates of airway colonization with Pseudomonas aeruginosa [9, 10]. Inflammatory markers, including IL-1β, are elevated in vitamin D-deficient individuals, suggesting a mechanistic link between malnutrition, inflammation, and physical decline [11].
These deficiencies, combined with ongoing systemic inflammation, contribute to sarcopenia and reduced resilience, increasing the likelihood of hospitalizations and functional dependency over time.

sec.title: Nutrition and Bronchiectasis
sec.p: Nutrition plays a critical role in the management and progression of bronchiectasis. Malnutrition, low body mass index (BMI), and micronutrient deficiencies are highly prevalent in this population and have been associated with increased morbidity, reduced lung function, higher hospitalization rates, and mortality. Emerging evidence supports the integration of nutritional assessment and targeted interventions into bronchiectasis care pathways to improve both systemic resilience and pulmonary outcomes.

sec.title: Nutritional Status and Disease Severity
sec.p: Malnutrition is highly prevalent in bronchiectasis and represents both a consequence and a contributor to disease progression. It is associated with systemic inflammation, impaired immune defenses, and reduced respiratory and skeletal muscle strength, all of which amplify functional decline and increase the risk of exacerbations and hospitalization.

Underweight status—defined as a body mass index (BMI) < 18.5 kg/m2—is a well-established surrogate marker of nutritional depletion. In an extensive US registry study, underweight patients demonstrated significantly lower FEV1, more frequent exacerbations, and higher mortality compared with those with normal or elevated BMI [2]. This phenotype is prevalent in older women and those with nontuberculous mycobacterial lung disease (NTM-LD), suggesting a distinct subgroup with heightened systemic vulnerability.

Several validated prognostic tools integrate BMI to assess disease severity and predict outcomes. The Bronchiectasis Severity Index (BSI) includes BMI, exacerbation frequency, hospitalization history, lung function, radiologic extent, microbiology, and comorbidities, highlighting the consistent association between low BMI and poor prognosis [12]. The BMI, age, cavity presence, erythrocyte sedimentation rate (ESR), and sex (BACES) score, explicitly developed for NTM-related bronchiectasis, links undernutrition and systemic inflammation with increased mortality risk [13]. The FEV1, age, chronic colonization by bacteria, extension, and dyspnea (FACED) score—while not including nutritional metrics—uses frailty-associated variables, such as age, dyspnea, and colonization with Pseudomonas aeruginosa, which may indirectly reflect the influence of nutritional status.

Importantly, BMI alone may not reflect true nutritional risk. Sarcopenia, or the progressive loss of skeletal muscle mass and strength, may coexist with normal or borderline BMI and remains underrecognized in this population. Sarcopenia impairs respiratory mechanics, reduces cough efficiency, and diminishes exercise tolerance, leading to more frequent exacerbations and hospitalizations [14].

Functional assessments—including handgrip strength, mid-arm circumference, gait speed, or dual-energy X-ray absorptiometry (DEXA)—offer more precise identification of lean mass loss. Though not yet widely implemented in bronchiectasis management, these tools may enable early detection of nutritionally vulnerable patients [15].

In addition, protein-energy malnutrition (PEM)—the inadequate intake of calories and protein relative to increased metabolic demands—is a frequent and often underrecognized contributor to muscle wasting in bronchiectasis. Chronic inflammation, elevated work of breathing, and appetite suppression during exacerbations exacerbate this negative energy balance. PEM further compromises immune function, reduces protein synthesis, and impairs tissue repair, compounding frailty and prolonging recovery. Guidelines for older adults with chronic disease recommend protein intake of 1.2–1.5 g/kg/day, though many bronchiectasis patients fail to meet these targets.

Taken together, these findings underscore the importance of early and comprehensive nutritional assessment. Incorporating both anthropometric indices and functional measures into routine care can improve risk stratification and inform personalized nutritional interventions to support long-term outcomes.

sec.title: Micronutrient Deficiencies
sec.p: Micronutrient deficiencies have been linked to systemic inflammation, impaired immune function, and disease progression in COPD and may play a similar role in bronchiectasis, though direct evidence remains limited. While macronutrient intake supports energy balance and body mass maintenance, micronutrients—including vitamin D, zinc, and antioxidants—are essential for maintaining mucosal integrity, modulating immunity, and protecting against oxidative damage [16].

sec.title: Vitamin D
sec.p: Vitamin D plays a pivotal role in regulating both innate and adaptive immune responses. It enhances macrophage and T-cell function and promotes the production of antimicrobial peptides such as cathelicidin (LL-37), which support mucosal defense in the respiratory tract [10]. Deficiency of vitamin D appears to be common in bronchiectasis, though most evidence linking it to reduced lung function, higher exacerbation rates, and infection risk.

In a landmark study, Chalmers et al. (2013) demonstrated that 21.4% of patients with bronchiectasis who had vitamin D deficiency were colonized with Pseudomonas aeruginosa, compared with only 3.6% of those with sufficient levels [9]. Additionally, patients with nontuberculous mycobacterial lung disease (NTM-LD) often exhibit significantly lower serum vitamin D concentrations than those with non-NTM bronchiectasis, correlating with increased disease severity and frailty burden [10]. Lower vitamin D levels have also been linked to elevated levels of pro-inflammatory cytokines such as interleukin-1β (IL-1β), further perpetuating systemic inflammation [11].

sec.title: Zinc
sec.p: Zinc is essential for neutrophil and T-cell function, cytokine regulation, and maintenance of epithelial barrier integrity. Its deficiency has been associated with impaired wound healing, increased susceptibility to respiratory infections, and dysregulated immune signaling [16]. Although direct evidence in bronchiectasis is limited, findings from COPD and tuberculosis populations suggest that zinc supplementation can enhance immune responsiveness and may reduce exacerbation frequency [17]. Given the overlapping inflammatory mechanisms across these conditions, zinc deficiency in bronchiectasis likely contributes to microbial persistence and chronic inflammation.

sec.title: Antioxidants (Vitamin C, Vitamin E, and Selenium)
sec.p: Chronic neutrophilic inflammation and recurrent infections in bronchiectasis result in high levels of reactive oxygen species (ROS), promoting oxidative lung injury. Antioxidants such as vitamin C, vitamin E, and selenium are crucial for neutralizing oxidative stress and preserving immune function [16]. Deficiencies in these antioxidants have been observed in malnourished individuals and are thought to exacerbate systemic inflammation, accelerate lung function decline, and increase vulnerability to infections. While interventional studies are sparse, limited observational evidence suggests a possible role for screening and addressing antioxidant deficiencies, particularly among patients with frequent exacerbations or inadequate nutritional intake.

sec.title: Clinical Considerations
sec.p: Routine screening for micronutrient status—especially vitamin D and zinc—is advisable in patients with frequent exacerbations, fatigue, low BMI, or clinical signs of malabsorption. Target serum 25(OH)D concentrations are generally considered adequate between 30 and 50 ng/mL (75 and 125 nmol/L) [18]. Empirical supplementation may be justified in high-risk populations, including the older, frail, or housebound individuals. Likewise, assessment of antioxidant and trace mineral levels should be integrated into nutritional evaluations, with input from registered dietitians to tailor supplementation on the basis of clinical need, comorbidities, and dietary intake.

sec.title: Nutritional Interventions
sec.p: Addressing malnutrition is a central goal in the nonpharmacologic management of bronchiectasis, particularly among patients with frailty, sarcopenia, or frequent exacerbations. Effective nutritional interventions aim to restore energy and protein balance, correct micronutrient deficiencies, and support immune and muscle function.

sec.title: Macronutrient Optimization
sec.p: Chronic inflammation, increased respiratory effort, and recurrent infections contribute to elevated energy expenditure in bronchiectasis. Simultaneously, reduced appetite and early satiety often lead to inadequate intake. Protein is especially critical for preserving lean body mass and promoting tissue repair. Current guidelines recommend 1.2–1.5 g/kg/day of protein for chronically ill or frail adults [14]. Small, frequent meals composed of energy-dense foods—such as legumes, dairy, soy, and healthy fats—can help patients meet these needs. In those with reduced oral intake, oral nutritional supplements (ONS) provide a practical strategy to augment protein and calorie intake. While randomized trials in bronchiectasis are limited, evidence from COPD and cystic fibrosis supports the benefits of high-protein supplementation on muscle strength, exercise tolerance, and weight gain. [10, 19]

sec.title: Micronutrient Repletion
sec.p: Micronutrient deficiencies are common and may impair immunity and recovery. Vitamin D plays a critical role in modulating immune responses and is frequently deficient in bronchiectasis patients, especially those with NTM-LD. Supplementation targeting serum 25(OH)D levels of 30–50 ng/mL (75–125 nmol/L) may reduce exacerbation risk and support musculoskeletal health [9, 18]. Although data specific to bronchiectasis are sparse, zinc, selenium, and antioxidant repletion—such as vitamins C and E—may also benefit patients with frequent infections or systemic inflammation [16].

sec.title: Personalized Nutrition Counseling
sec.p: Dietitian-led interventions are essential for tailoring dietary strategies to individual needs, comorbidities, and symptoms. Registered dietitians can assess energy requirements, identify specific nutrient gaps, and accommodate cultural or gastrointestinal factors. Ongoing monitoring using tools such as the Mini Nutritional Assessment (MNA), Subjective Global Assessment (SGA), or Malnutrition Universal Screening Tool (MUST) helps track response to therapy and guide adjustments [5, 15]. Integrating nutritional support with pulmonary rehabilitation can further enhance outcomes; timed protein intake around resistance exercise has been shown to improve muscle protein synthesis and functional recovery [14].

sec.title: Physical Activity, Sedentary Behavior, and Deconditioning
sec.p: Patients with chronic lung diseases tend to become more inactive to avoid exertional dyspnea and leg fatigue. In an attempt to minimize respiratory discomfort and energy expenditure, patients often self-adapt to a more sedentary lifestyle, which ultimately contributes to muscle atrophy and loss of physical independence. Over time, this behavioral adaptation fosters profound muscle disuse, systemic deconditioning, and a progressive loss of functional reserve.

Physical activity (PA) is defined as any body movement that requires energy expenditure and is categorized into three groups: (1) light PA requires 1.5–3 metabolic equivalents of task (METs) and is rated 2–4 on a perceived exertion scale, (2) moderate-intensity PA requires 3- < 6 METs and is rated 5–6 on perceived exertion scale, and (3) vigorous PA requires ≥ 6 METs and is rated 7–8 on a perceived exertion scale. The aging process itself will result in a natural decline in pulmonary function, and studies have shown that PA may delay the decline of pulmonary function occurring in middle-aged and elderly patients [20] (Pelkonen et al., 2003). There are multiple factors, including airway obstruction and muscular weakness, that will lead to an imbalance between ventilatory capacity and demand, resulting in PA limitations. Improved PA is associated with higher functional capacity, slower decline in pulmonary function, and lower mortality rate. It is essential to determine PA level accurately to identify appropriate management strategies, using a combination of objective and subjective measures.

The World Health Organization defines sedentary behavior (SB) as behavior that consumes less than or equal to 1.5 metabolic equivalents of task (METs). It is well established that SB has negative consequences on general health. Physical inactivity and SB are widespread in bronchiectasis and contribute directly to frailty. Objective measurements using accelerometry and pedometry reveal that many patients with bronchiectasis average only 5200–5800 steps per day—well below healthy norms—and spend more than 9 h per day in sedentary behavior. Patients who ambulate ≤ 6290 steps per day or spend ≥ 7.8 h per day in sedentary behavior were noted to have increased risk of hospital admission for bronchiectasis exacerbation at 1-year follow up [3]. Notably, ≥ 7.8 h per day of sedentary behavior was associated with a 5.9-fold higher risk of hospital admission within 1 year after adjustment for relevant confounding factors, including age, gender, Pseudomonas aeruginosa colonization, and hospitalization in the 12 months prior to the study [3].

Prolonged inactivity accelerates muscle atrophy and systemic inflammation, suppressing anabolic pathways such as mTOR and impairing mitochondrial function [21]. These physiological changes lead to reduced skeletal muscle activity, decreased muscle strength, decreased aerobic capacity, and impaired gait stability. During periods of extended immobility, including exacerbations and hospital stays, these effects become even more pronounced. Bed rest can cause muscle strength to decline by several percentage points per day, particularly in the lower limbs and postural muscles, increasing fall risk, prolonging recovery, and raising the likelihood of hospitalization.

In addition to sarcopenic muscle loss, older adults tend to lose lean tissue more rapidly than younger individuals during periods of prolonged physical inactivity. While protein supplementation has the potential to maintain some muscle protein synthesis, this supplementation should ideally be part of integrated countermeasure regimens managed by a multidisciplinary team that includes balanced nutrition, exercise, and pharmacologic interventions when appropriate [22].

sec.title: Benefits of Physical Activity
sec.p: Structured physical activity, particularly within the framework of pulmonary rehabilitation (PR), remains a cornerstone nonpharmacologic intervention in bronchiectasis. PR programs have demonstrated improvements in cardiorespiratory fitness, skeletal muscle strength, and health-related quality of life (HRQoL), thereby mitigating the progressive deconditioning and frailty common in this population [23, 24]. Multimodal programs incorporating aerobic and resistance training consistently yield clinically meaningful gains in functional capacity. For example, Lee et al. (2014) reported a 62 m improvement in 6-min walk distance (6MWD) and enhanced peak VO2 following an 8 week exercise program in patients with bronchiectasis and NTM-LD [25].

Complementary strategies such as resistance training and inspiratory muscle training (IMT) further enhance physical performance, particularly in older adults and those with sarcopenia. IMT has been associated with improvements in inspiratory muscle strength, dyspnea relief, and HRQoL [26, 27]. Although spirometric parameters such as FEV1 typically show modest change, gains in functional independence and exercise tolerance are well documented [28].

Improvements in HRQoL are also reflected in validated patient-reported outcomes, including the St. George’s Respiratory Questionnaire (SGRQ) and the Bronchiectasis Health Questionnaire (BHQ), with notable benefits in breathlessness, fatigue, mood, and sleep quality, even in the absence of spirometric change [25, 26].

Despite the evidence base, adherence to PR remains suboptimal, with fewer than half of referred patients completing programs. Factors such as exertional dyspnea, persistent cough, multimorbidity (e.g., osteoarthritis, cardiovascular disease), and psychological distress frequently hinder participation [24, 26].

To address these barriers, home-based and telehealth-enabled PR models are being adopted. A randomized controlled trial by Lee et al. demonstrated comparable outcomes between home-based and traditional center-based PR [29]. Telerehabilitation platforms that incorporate video consultations, mobile apps, and wearable sensors have shown promise in improving functional performance and patient satisfaction in related conditions, with the added potential benefit of enhancing accessibility [26].

Furthermore, hospital-at-home innovations such as the TeleCOVID trial demonstrated that remote monitoring, virtual follow-ups, and wearable-supported surveillance reduce emergency department visits and readmissions following respiratory illness [30].

Ultimately, physical inactivity is a modifiable risk factor. Walking fewer than 6290 steps/day or spending more than 7.8 h/day sedentary independently predicts hospitalization [3]. Therefore, deploying personalized, adaptable PR strategies—whether through conventional, home-based, or digital modalities—is vital to preserving autonomy, minimizing frailty, and improving outcomes in bronchiectasis.

sec.title: Mental Health in Bronchiectasis
sec.p: Mood disturbances are an underrecognized yet clinically important consequence of chronic lung disease, including bronchiectasis. Anxiety and depression affect a substantial proportion of individuals with bronchiectasis, with prevalence estimates ranging from 31% to 55% for anxiety and 14% to 34% for depression [31, 32]. Common symptoms include persistent fatigue, poor concentration, and low mood, which can significantly impact daily functioning and overall well-being.

Elevated anxiety and depression scores are strongly correlated with worse health-related quality of life (HRQoL). Higher depression and anxiety scores were observed in patients older than 25 years and were associated with more frequent exacerbations [33]. Notably, after adjusting for age, gender, and key clinical variables—such as spirometry, daily sputum production, and bronchiectasis etiology—both depression and anxiety remained independent predictors of reduced HRQoL [34].

Furthermore, mood impairment is closely linked to fatigue and perceived health status. Patients with elevated anxiety and depression report greater tiredness and functional limitation, even in the absence of more severe objective disease findings. Neither anxiety nor depression scores were correlated with the radiographic extent of bronchiectasis on CT, echoing prior studies that also found no relationship between anatomical disease severity and patient-reported health status.

Interestingly, the psychological burden appears to affect patients through distinct mechanisms: non-somatic features of depression are often tied to dyspnea and reduced exercise tolerance, whereas the drivers of anxiety are more complex and less clearly linked to physical parameters. Nonetheless, impaired mood consistently corresponds to worse subjective health perceptions, highlighting the importance of routine mental health assessment and support in bronchiectasis care.

sec.title: Limitations in Current Management Strategies
sec.p: Frailty, malnutrition, and inactivity form a self-reinforcing cycle that worsens outcomes in bronchiectasis. Chronic inflammation fuels muscle loss and immune dysfunction, malnutrition weakens respiratory muscles and overall health, and sedentary behavior accelerates physical decline. Together, these factors increase hospitalization rates, slow recovery, and reduce survival.

Management of frailty in bronchiectasis has historically drawn from broader chronic lung disease and geriatric care models, as disease-specific guidelines remain lacking. Ultimately, multifaceted pulmonary rehabilitation remains the cornerstone of therapy. It has been shown to improve exercise tolerance, reduce breathlessness, and enhance health-related quality of life, even among frail patients. However, participation and completion rates among frail individuals remain low due to access barriers and functional limitations.

Given that bronchiectasis disproportionately affects older adults and women—groups at higher baseline risk for frailty—a shift toward multidisciplinary, patient-centered care is urgently needed. Standardizing frailty screening and embedding multimodal interventions into bronchiectasis care pathways may mitigate disease progression, improve quality of life, and reduce hospitalizations.

Nutritional interventions are essential and often involve targeted caloric and protein repletion. Screening tools such as the Mini Nutritional Assessment help identify patients at risk and guide appropriate interventions. Beyond addressing macronutrient needs, correction of micronutrient deficiencies, particularly vitamin D, can support muscle function and immune health.

A multidisciplinary approach is widely recommended. Effective management requires collaboration among pulmonologists, geriatricians, physical therapists, and dietitians to address the multifactorial needs of patients. While bronchiectasis-specific frailty tools are not available, practical instruments such as the Fried Frailty Phenotype and the Clinical Frailty Scale are increasingly used in clinical settings to guide care.

sec.title: Policy Recommendation: Ensuring Access to Pulmonary Rehabilitation and Nutritional Interventions for Bronchiectasis
sec.p: We recommend that health policymakers formally recognize pulmonary rehabilitation (PR) and nutritional interventions as essential, reimbursable components of standard care for patients with bronchiectasis. Despite strong evidence supporting their efficacy in improving functional status, reducing exacerbations, mitigating frailty, and enhancing quality of life, access to PR and nutritional care remains limited and inconsistent. To address this gap, public and private insurers—including Medicare and Medicaid—should expand coverage to include comprehensive PR services (center-based, home-based, and telerehabilitation models) and nutrition counseling by registered dietitians. Clinical guidelines should mandate routine frailty screening and nutritional assessment for all patients with moderate to severe bronchiectasis. Reimbursement policies must support the delivery of individualized exercise training, protein supplementation, and micronutrient repletion when clinically indicated. Integrating these nonpharmacologic therapies into routine care pathways will align policy with the current evidence base, reduce long-term healthcare utilization, and promote equitable, patient-centered care for this high-risk population.

sec.title: Conclusion
sec.p: Bronchiectasis is a complex, heterogeneous respiratory condition that demands a multidimensional approach to care. There is no typical bronchiectasis patient as the disorder can develop due to many differing underlying etiologies, with four key common aspects in disease pathophysiology: infection, inflammation, dysfunction of mucociliary clearance, and structural lung damage. While pharmacologic therapies are essential in managing airway infection and inflammation, growing evidence underscores the critical role of nonpharmacologic strategies—particularly nutrition and exercise—in addressing systemic consequences such as frailty, sarcopenia, physical inactivity, and reduced quality of life. Addressing frailty in bronchiectasis demands early recognition, standardized screening, and integration of multimodal interventions into care pathways. By focusing on nutrition, physical rehabilitation, and multidisciplinary collaboration, clinicians can disrupt this trajectory and improve resilience, function, and quality of life for affected patients.

This review highlights the significant burden of malnutrition and physical deconditioning in patients with bronchiectasis, and the strong associations between low body mass index, sedentary behavior, and adverse clinical outcomes, including hospitalizations and mortality. Despite this, pulmonary rehabilitation and nutritional interventions remain underutilized and inconsistently integrated into standard care. Multidisciplinary team involvement, including pulmonologists, dietitians, physiotherapists, and psychologists, is essential to assess, monitor, and manage the diverse needs of this population.

Validated tools now allow clinicians to screen for frailty, malnutrition, and inactivity, enabling early intervention and personalized care planning. Evidence-based interventions—including high-protein, calorically adequate diets and structured endurance and resistance training—can improve functional status, reduce symptom burden, and enhance health-related quality of life. Digital health platforms and telerehabilitation offer scalable solutions to extend the reach of these services, especially for frail patients.

ack.title: Acknowledgements
ack.p: The authors wish to thank Dr. Naman Chaudary and the American College of Chest Physicians for their support of research in bronchiectasis and support of national initiatives. The views, information, or content, and conclusions presented do not necessarily reflect the official position or policy of, nor should any official endorsement be inferred on the part of the Clinical Center, National Heart and Lung Institute, the National Institutes of Health (NIH), or the Department of Health and Human Services. This research was supported in part by the Intramural Research Program of the NIH

notes.title: Author Contributions
notes.p: Chetana Pendkar: conceptualization, drafting and revising of the manuscript, and integration of reviewer/editor feedback. Amarpreet K. Ahluwalia: critical revision of the manuscript, clinical expertise, and intellectual input.

notes.title: Funding
notes.p: No funding or sponsorship was received for this study or publication of this article.

notes.title: Conflict of interest
notes.p: Chetana Pendkar and Amarpreet K. Ahluwalia have nothing to disclose.

notes.title: Ethical Approval
notes.p: This article is based on previously conducted studies and does not contain any new studies with human participants or animals performed by any of the authors.
